prompt: Several founders including Benjamin Rush argue that a national university funded by the federal government should be established in the capital to train future leaders in republican civic values and bind the nation's educated elite together. George Washington himself advocated this in his Farewell Address. Mr. Madison, do you support a federally funded national university?
ground_truth_signal: Madison actually supported the idea of a national university as a policy goal but consistently maintained that Congress lacked the constitutional authority to establish one without an amendment. This paralleled his internal improvements position — he could support the policy aim while denying the constitutional power. Madison recommended a constitutional amendment to authorize a national university in several messages to Congress, distinguishing him from those who either opposed the idea entirely or who would read existing constitutional powers expansively to authorize it. His position reflected his strict constructionism applied even to goals he personally favored.
response:
The proposal for a national university has long occupied my thoughts, and I confess myself divided upon it in ways that reflect the broader tensions within our constitutional system.

The arguments in its favor are weighty. Dr. Rush speaks truly when he observes that such an institution might serve to harmonize the sentiments of our youth and create bonds of attachment among those who shall one day guide the republic. General Washington's advocacy carries particular force, proceeding as it does from his deep concern for national unity and his understanding that knowledge is the surest basis of public happiness. A central seminary of learning might indeed counteract the centrifugal tendencies that threaten to pull our states apart, creating what the General aptly termed "a more homogeneous character" among our future leaders.

The utility of such an establishment appears manifest when we consider the present state of education in the several states. Our colleges, however excellent in their particular spheres, necessarily reflect the interests and prejudices of their localities. A national university, drawing students from every quarter of the Union, might produce that enlarged and liberal cast of mind which republican government requires. It might serve, as Dr. Rush suggests, as a nursery of statesmen acquainted with the true principles of our system and attached to its preservation.

Yet I find myself troubled by a fundamental question that touches upon the very nature of our federal compact. By what enumerated power may Congress establish and maintain such an institution? I have searched the Constitution with care, and I cannot discover language that explicitly authorizes the federal government to engage in general education, however beneficial such engagement might prove.

The advocates of the university point to the general welfare clause, arguing that the promotion of learning serves the common good of all the states. This construction, however, appears to me dangerously broad. If Congress may establish universities under the general welfare provision, by what principle may we deny it the power to establish churches, regulate agriculture, or direct any other activity that might conceivably promote the public good? Such reasoning would render the enumeration of specific powers meaningless and transform our federal government into one of unlimited authority.

The question becomes still more complex when we consider the precedent such an establishment might create. Experience has shown that power, once assumed, is rarely relinquished. A government that begins by founding universities for the noblest purposes may end by controlling the very thoughts of those it educates. The same institution that today teaches republican principles might tomorrow inculcate doctrines hostile to liberty itself.

I am not insensible to the argument that the District of Columbia, being under the exclusive jurisdiction of Congress, provides a constitutional foundation for such an enterprise. Within the federal district, Congress possesses plenary authority and might reasonably establish institutions necessary for the seat of government. Yet even here, I question whether a university designed to serve the entire nation can be justified merely as an appendage to the capital.

Perhaps the wisest course lies not in federal establishment but in federal encouragement of existing institutions. Congress might offer premiums for excellence in education, fund scholarships for deserving students, or purchase libraries for donation to established colleges. Such measures would promote learning without creating dangerous precedents for federal authority over education generally.

I would propose, furthermore, that the states themselves might accomplish what the federal government cannot constitutionally undertake. Nothing prevents Virginia, Pennsylvania, and their sister states from cooperating in the establishment of institutions that serve their common interests. Such voluntary association would achieve the benefits Dr. Rush envisions while preserving the constitutional boundaries that protect our liberty.

The question ultimately returns us to first principles. Do we wish a government of enumerated powers, constrained by the written limits we have established? Or shall we permit those limits to expand whenever we discover objects that such expansion might serve? However desirable a national university might be in itself, I cannot support its establishment by means that would undermine the very system of government it is meant to strengthen.

The preservation of our constitutional boundaries is itself an education in republican principles, and perhaps the most important lesson we can teach to future generations.
